comment_text: AI generated political ads should be banned in my opinion, but this disclosure regulation is a good start at the very least.

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mr. alva griffis

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.

The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual's appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstancei, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.

The FCC has no statutory authority to police the content of political advertising. Such an attempt raises serious constitutional concerns and undermines the Federal Election Commission's exclusive jurisdiction in this area. 

This proposal threatens to create inconsistent and confusing rules that will leave voters uncertain about the authenticity of political ads, ultimately benefiting certain political interests by suppressing opposing voices.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Penny Bowdish

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mr. Vincent Puma

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC’s proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

Sincerely,
Mr. Richard Salher

Sincerely,

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mrs. Denise Grate

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.
The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual’s appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstance, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.
The FCC has no statutory authority to police the content of political advertising. Such an attempt raises serious constitutional concerns and undermines the Federal Election Commission's exclusive jurisdiction in this area.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mr. Bill Burris

The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
carissa fairchild

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.
The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual’s appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstance, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.
The FCC has no statutory authority to police the content of political advertising. Such an attempt raises serious constitutional concerns and undermines the Federal Election Commission's exclusive jurisdiction in this area. 
This proposal threatens to create inconsistent and confusing rules that will leave voters uncertain about the authenticity of political ads, ultimately benefiting certain political interests by suppressing opposing voices.
I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mr. Martin Berry

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC’s proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

Sincerely,
Mrs. Linda Bonner

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mrs. Joan Slagle

Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mrs. Cathleen Dale

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC’s proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

Sincerely,
Mr. Stephen Calvert

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mrs. Marion A Raymond

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.
The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual’s appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstance, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.
The FCC has no statutory authority to police the content of political advertising. Such an attempt raises serious constitutional concerns and undermines the Federal Election Commission's exclusive jurisdiction in this area. 
This proposal threatens to create inconsistent and confusing rules that will leave voters uncertain about the authenticity of political ads, ultimately benefiting certain political interests by suppressing opposing voices.
I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

This proposal threatens to create inconsistent and confusing rules that will leave voters uncertain about the authenticity of political ads, ultimately benefiting certain political interests by suppressing opposing voices.
I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mr. Kenneth Isaacs

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mr. Dean Kauffman

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
John Willse

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC’s proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

Sincerely,
Mr. Joe Scarboro

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC’s proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

Sincerely,
MERRILL Arnold

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
James Jordan

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.
The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual’s appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstance, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.
The FCC has no statutory authority to police the content of political advertising. Such an attempt raises serious constitutional concerns and undermines the Federal Election Commission's exclusive jurisdiction in this area. 
This proposal threatens to create inconsistent and confusing rules that will leave voters uncertain about the authenticity of political ads, ultimately benefiting certain political interests by suppressing opposing voices.
I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mr. Doug Van Horn

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mr. Eli Dumitru

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC’s proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

Sincerely,
Mr. JAMES FREDETTE

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Marianne Richard

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.
The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual’s appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstance, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.
The FCC has no statutory authority to police the content of political advertising. Such an attempt raises serious constitutional concerns and undermines the Federal Election Commission's exclusive jurisdiction in this area. 
This proposal threatens to create inconsistent and confusing rules that will leave voters uncertain about the authenticity of political ads, ultimately benefiting certain political interests by suppressing opposing voices.
I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Duane Kridle

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mr. RICK JONES

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.
The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual’s appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstance, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Ms. Ann Krusec

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Thomas Stenson

Your proposed regulations for AI-generated content in political advertisements would cause significant confusion and directly conflict with established laws. These regulations are not only unnecessary but also detrimental to our democratic process.
The useless definition of AI in the proposed rule ("an image, audio, or video that has been generated using computational technology or other machine-based system that depicts an individual’s appearance, speech, or conduct, or an event, circumstance, or situation") could be applied to literally any modern political ad, creating an opening for censorship and manipulation.
The FCC has no statutory authority to police the content of political advertising. Such an attempt raises serious constitutional concerns and undermines the Federal Election Commission's exclusive jurisdiction in this area. 
This proposal threatens to create inconsistent and confusing rules that will leave voters uncertain about the authenticity of political ads, ultimately benefiting certain political interests by suppressing opposing voices.
I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Nicholas Maloof

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Alan Zeidman

Knowing that an ad has been generated or altered by AI helps viewers and listeners critically evaluate the information being presented and discern whether it is authentic or manipulated. Also, AI-generated content can be highly persuasive and tailored to exploit emotional or cognitive biases. By requiring disclosure, the rule aims to mitigate the risk of AI being used to manipulate public opinion in deceptive ways. Furthermore, political ads can have a substantial impact on elections and public policy. Ensuring that AI-generated content in these ads is clearly identified helps maintain the integrity of the political process and prevents potential misuse of AI technologies to mislead voters. Lastly, this rule could serve as a model for other forms of media and platforms, including social media, where AI-generated content is also prevalent. By establishing a standard for disclosure, it paves the way for broader regulations that can address the challenges posed by AI in various contexts.

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mr. Matt Hobson

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mr. William Blackman III

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mr. Douglas Riddle

It's nearly impossible to discern fake from real images. Please stop this terrible thing from happening.

Fabrications intentionally exploit the "Anchoring Effect," or the well known psychological tendency to give greater importance to information that is presented as new - whether in print or as images, audio recordings or audiovisual constructs.

This privileges fabrications with importance outsized of their truth or falsehood. Thus any effort to refute already requires doubled or trebled effort.

Artificial intelligence wedded to the purpose of fabrication to interfere with free choice in the marketplace is already inimical. But allowing it to manipulate the democratic process of elections would be catastrophic.

This technology automatically places real persons at a disadvantage, requiring them to question whether the eerie similitude of what is put before them is "real" or not. And in the contexts of politics and the functioning of our democracy - where timing is critical in decision-making that has consequences lasting years - is an inherently fatal conflict.

Therefore, the best solution is to institute a blanket ban on any and all content generated by or associated with artificial intelligence in campaign ads. Barring that, nothing less than requiring full disclosure of its use is absolutely necessary.

This proposal must contain harsh penalties and fines for those who break or ignore the proposed regulations.

I am writing to express my serious concerns regarding the FCC's proposed rulemaking for AI-generated content in political advertisements. Alarmingly, in its attempt to justify this overreach, the FCC has revived sweeping, decades-old agency precedents. Specifically, the agency references a policy statement from the 1960s, which announced the FCC’s expectation for broadcasters to “take all reasonable measures to eliminate any false, misleading, or deceptive matter” under threat of license revocation. This policy statement suggests a regulatory authority far beyond simple disclosure requirements, effectively positioning the FCC as the nation’s speech police.

Such an expansive interpretation of the FCC’s authority is deeply troubling. The regulatory power claimed by the FCC today would enable the agency to operate as an arbiter of truth in political speech, a role that is both inappropriate and unconstitutional. This overreach threatens to stifle free speech and disrupt the democratic process by imposing undue burdens on broadcasters.

Your proposed AI definition is so broad that any political ad targeted for censorship -- including by corrupt "third-party" fact-checkers -- could be implicated.

Congress has clearly defined the boundaries of the FCC’s authority, which do not include acting as a gatekeeper for political speech. The revival of such far-reaching precedents is an alarming step towards undermining free speech and the democratic process.

I strongly urge you to immediately withdraw the proposed rule.

Sincerely,
Mr. Louis Giannetto

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC’s authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC’s proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC’s role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.

None of the laws cited in the proposed rule provide the agency with the sweeping authority it claims over political speech. The Supreme Court has decisively ruled against such novel interpretations that lack clear congressional authorization.

In light of these considerations, I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of the proposed rulemaking.

Sincerely,
Mrs. RITA NORBY

Congress has explicitly granted the Federal Election Commission (FEC)—not the FCC—the exclusive statutory authority to interpret, administer, and enforce the Federal Election Campaign Act (52 U.S.C. § 30106(b)). This includes the authority to establish disclosures for political communications on television and radio (52 U.S.C. § 30120). The courts have consistently recognized the FEC’s unique role in regulating political disclosures, even when other agencies have attempted to circumvent or supplement its rules, concluding that “the FEC is the exclusive administrative arbiter of questions concerning the name identifications and disclaimers” for political communications (Galliano v. United States Postal Service, 836 F.2d 1362, 1368–70 (D.C. Cir. 1988)).

Currently, the FEC is actively considering the very issues implicated by your proposed rule, and legislators in Congress are engaged as well. A DNC-backed petition has asked the FEC to impose new regulations on AI-generated political speech—a proposal that mirrors the FCC’s current initiative—before voters hit the ballot boxes this fall (See Petition of Public Citizen, July 13, 2023; Comments of Democratic National Committee, REG 2023-02, Oct. 16, 2023).

The FEC Chairman has warned that the FCC’s proposal would encroach upon the FEC’s exclusive jurisdiction, directly conflict with existing laws and regulations, and sow chaos among political campaigns for the upcoming election.

I strongly urge the immediate withdrawal of these proposed regulations.

Sincerely,
Mr. Dean Johnson

High quality deepfakes can realistically depict people saying or doing things that did not occur. By requiring a clear disclosure every time AI is used to make an ad, you can prevent political actors from deceiving the public.

Your proposal is a strong step to ensure transparency and the integrity of our democratic process. Please adopt it without delay. Thank you for your time.

Given recent advances in artificial intelligence, I’m concerned that candidates and issue campaigners will have an open invitation to deceive voters unless you step in. High quality deepfakes can realistically depict people saying or doing things that did not occur. By requiring a clear disclosure every time AI is used to make an ad, you can prevent political actors from deceiving the public.

Your proposal is a strong step to ensure transparency and the integrity of our democratic process. Please adopt it without delay. Thank you for your time.

We urge the FCC Commissioners to adopt the proposal in “The Disclosure and Transparency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content in Political Advertisements” as soon as possible.  

Because of recent advances in artificial intelligence, it is extremely concerning to us that candidates and political issue campaigners will have an open field to deceive voters such as us, unless the FCC steps in. High quality deepfakes can realistically depict people saying or doing things that did not occur.  As an example, just this evening (9/18/2024) we watched a report on Alex Wagner's MSNBC program:  a television ad was shown, ostensibly provided by KBSF, a station in San Francisco, featuring a pitifully sad black woman in a wheelchair, reporting in clear accented English how her irreversible paralysis was caused by a car accident;  showing X-rays taken of the victim's various dreadful injuries, and a photo of the accident; then reporting that two men had come to her hospital room the next day to tell her and her mother that her awful injuries were caused by Kamala Harris, “a very powerful woman.”  It was an extremely believable report.  Turns out, however, there is no such TV station in San Francisco, no paralyzed woman, the X-rays were taken 7 years before the alleged accident, and the photo of the accident itself was from an occurrence in Guam.  This, according to the report on Alex Wagner's program, is believed to be part of a Russian election-interference effort. These AI ads, observed hundreds of thousands of times, constitute frightening “misinformation super-spreaders.”  

By requiring a clear disclosure every time AI is used to create an ad, you can prevent political actors from deceiving the public in ways that this ad and others like it could very easily do.  It's only human nature to expect the worst of people seen as “the other,” or to believe descriptions of issues that might be totally contrived. Many well-meaning voters believe what they see on TV.   In a time of political division, with threats of and/or actual violence, such fakes cause irreparable societal damage.  

This FCC proposal is a strong step to ensure transparency and to help to preserve the integrity of  the democratic process in our nation.  We urge you Commissioners to adopt it without delay, and we thank you for giving our concerns, and those of hundreds of thousands of other citizens, your most serious consideration.

In the Matter of Disclosure and Transparency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content in Political Advertisements
  
MB Docket No. 24-211


Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the Federal Communications Commission’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on “Disclosure and Transparency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content in Political Advertisements.” I am Director of the Center on Technology Policy at New York University. The views expressed here do not represent those of New York University. 

The NPRM, among other things, would require broadcasters to provide a standardized on-air disclosure that certain ads contain AI-generated content. This label requirement is based on the assumption that such AI content labels are helpful.

We tested that assumption in two pieces of recent research. Our review of academic literature on other kinds of warning and informational labels revealed that labels can work, but only a little, only sometimes, and often have costs. Our findings suggest that in many situations, the costs of AI content labels may outweigh any benefits.

We also conducted what is, as far as we know, the first experiment testing the impact of AI disclaimers on political ads. This experiment indicated that the labels now required by Michigan and Florida broadly hurt candidates that used generative AI in their ads largely independent of the content of the ad. The experiment also demonstrates that label wording matters: seemingly small changes to the language can have significant effects.

These two pieces of research offer a series of lessons for policymakers who seek to require AI labels. Several of these lessons directly apply to the NPRM’s request for comments. We explain further for each relevant lesson below. 

Key AI Labeling Lessons for the FCC’s Proceeding
Below we discuss our five most relevant findings on the value of AI labeling from the two pieces of research. For each relevant lesson, we identify which NPRM questions it applies to, and how. 

1. AI labels hurt candidates who use generative AI, irrespective of the content of the ad. Policymakers must weigh the costs of requiring labels with the potential benefits.

When shown AI labels, respondents in our experiment generally rated candidates less trustworthy and less appealing, candidates’ ads less accurate, and indicated that they were less likely to approve and more likely to flag or report candidates’ ads on social media. 

At the same time, labels appeared to result in a “backfire effect” for candidates creating attack ads. While labels lowered user assessments of candidates sponsoring attack ads, they did not impact how users assessed the candidates attacked in negative ads. Absent labels, users rated the attacking and the attacked candidates similarly. With labels, however, users rate attacked candidates better than those doing the attacking. 

We also observed that labels were more likely to limit the impact of what are usually the most effective types of ads. Existing research underscores that political ads have minimal impact which candidate voters choose, but ads can have more impact on turn-out and donations. Therefore, we wanted to understand how political affiliation impacted the effect of AI labels to see if labels’ effects were stronger or weaker for voters assessing candidates of their own party--such as in fundraising or turn-out appeals. We saw evidence that AI labels damaged viewers’ assessments of candidates from their own political parties and of candidates with no political affiliation. In contrast, AI labels had no meaningful impact on viewers’ assessments of candidates from opposed political parties, mostly likely because their assessments of those candidates were already low. 

There is still much that we do not know about how candidates are using generative AI in campaigns. However, some have suggested that while these tools could increase the amount of deceptive, false content, most campaigns will use the technology to create ads faster and for less money. This means that generative AI tools could provide disproportionate benefit to smaller, poorer campaigns. Therefore, the negative impact of AI disclaimer labels could also disproportionately impact those same smaller, poorer campaigns. 

Paragraph 12 of NPRM asks for comments on if and how NPRM would serve the public interest. These findings indicate that NPRM may not only have negative consequences for political candidates, but that those impacts might impact candidates unequally. 


2. The wording and design of labels matter. Policymakers should provide best practices on the design of AI content labels to maximize their benefits.

Both our and other’s research on this topic indicates that small changes to the design or wording of labels can have large impacts on labels’ impact. We saw notable differences in the impact and effects of the label now required by Michigan on political ads with deceptive use of AI: “This video has been manipulated by technical means and depicts speech or conduct that did not occur,” and the label required in Florida on any political ad that uses generative AI: “This video was created in whole or in part with the use of generative artificial intelligence.”

While there is growing empirical testing of different AI labels, there remains much we do not know. Before standardizing label language and design, as suggested in paragraph 16 of the NPRM, policymakers should support additional research to ensure label design maximizes benefits.


3. Policymakers should conduct assessments after establishing new AI content label requirements.

I applaud the inclusion of cost-benefit analysis prior to implementation in paragraph 35 of the proposed rule, however, there is value in also assessing the impact and effectiveness of labels once deployed. There is a long history of well intentioned policy having unintended consequences; policy should not only build in cost-benefit analysis while development, but also monitoring and evaluation to understand the true impact and effectiveness once deployed. Furthermore, regulation should permit revision or modifications to requirements pending the results of these assessments or evaluations. 


4. Policymakers should combine AI content labels with other interventions.

While we observed some significant effects of generative AI labels, effect sizes were generally small. At the same time, 26% of respondents shown the Florida label and 37% of respondents shown the Michigan label did not remember seeing the labels. Our experiment likely overestimates the attention that viewers will give to labels and to ads in the wild. 

Moreover, many of the respondents who observed disclaimers disagreed about their meaning. We also asked respondents to identify all of the technologies that might have been used to produce ads they saw. First, absent labels, few subjects identified that ads were created with generative AI. Even when exposed to a label that said the ad was created through “technical means,” less than a quarter of respondents identified that the ads were created with generative AI. These data confirm that voters have limited ability to detect differences in editing or production techniques, and that unless labels specifically identify that generative AI was used, respondents may first assume other, more traditional methods were employed. 

Given the effect size, that a sizable percentage of subjects did not remember seeing labels, and disagreements about the meaning of labels, policymakers should consider combining AI labeling requirements with other interventions. 


5. When asked about policy solutions, voters were most supportive of requiring labels on all political ads that contain generative AI.

In addition to asking subjects to assess candidates and ads, we also asked them to provide their agreement to three policy interventions regarding AI and political ads: requiring labels on all political ads with genAI, requiring labels only on political ads with deceptive uses of genAI, banning all generative AI in political ads. Of the three options, respondents were most supportive of requiring labels on all ads that contain generative AI. Notably, respondents expressed the least support for states requiring disclaimers only on political ads that contain deceptive uses of generative AI, the policy solution most commonly adopted by state governments. While most respondents likely had little familiarity with the legal or policy context, the question does provide some insight into how voters are included to approach these regulations. 

Conclusion
The NPRM’s proposal to impose labeling requirements on all political ads that contain generative AI content must consider that existing academic research indicates that while labels can work, they often have small effects, are heavily dependent on context, and may impose significant costs. Before establishing new labeling requirements, it is imperative that the FCC draws on insights from rigorous empirical analysis, both of the likely benefits and harms, but also how to maximize the benefits and minimize the harms. Or research underscores that in some contexts, the harms of labels may outweigh the benefits. Given our findings that AI labels are likely to reduce trust in and appeal of candidates regardless of how they use generative AI, indicates this might be the case for political ads as well.

I've been in the computer industry for nearly 5 decades, and seen it go through many growing pains. The latest is the wave of "Artificial Intelligence" (AI) that is sweeping through the industry. Hardly a day goes by that I don't get an invitation to inject AI into my development process. The problem, though, is that it's far from intelligent. Intelligence includes a conscience, which computer programs simply don't have.
There are many who use this tool - and AI is a TOOL - as a means to an artificial end. The video and gaming industry has used various software tools to change the look and feel of their environments for decades. Now, it's as if it's been put on steroids, and is being used for nefarious purposes: false political advertising. The recent advertisement showing Taylor Swift purportedly endorsing Donald Trump is just one example.
Permitting these deceptions to pollute the minds of the voters is unconscionable. Truth in advertising - especially when it comes to who is to represent us in the halls of power and politics - is of paramount importance. The FCC used to have a "Fairness Doctrine" which allowed opposing views to by presented on a (pretty much) equal footing, using real people.
We need to keep the spirit of honesty and truth alive in this country, particularly in politics. That is why I urge the FCC to adopt its proposal on the Disclosure and Transparency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content in Political Advertisements as soon as possible. Your proposal is a strong step to ensure transparency and the integrity of our democratic process. Please adopt it without delay.
Thank you for your time and consideration.

I am writing to express my concerns regarding your proposed rule for AI-generated content in political advertisements. In light of the Supreme Court's decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo, it is clear that the FCC does not possess the authority to enact this proposed rule.

Agencies cannot extend their regulatory reach beyond the specific authority granted by Congress. The FCC's authority over candidate ads is limited to ensuring that broadcasters provide candidates with equal access. The FCC's proposed regulations exceed this narrow mandate and encroach on the jurisdiction of the Federal Election Commission (FEC).

Congress intentionally restricted the FCC's role over political ads to specific, targeted matters. This limitation was designed to prevent the kind of overreach that the Commission is now attempting.
